items.title: Does Pauline Hanson's claim of growing support in the ACT pass the pub test?

children.content: The conventional wisdom is that Canberra is a largely left-leaning city, but the national capital is now among places in One Nation's sights as support for the party surges across the country.

items.title: Hikers lost near Kosciuszko found by artificial intelligence drone

children.content: Rescuers were able to locate the hikers less than five hours after they were reported missing.

image.altText: A grey image with a red dot.

items.title: Murder trial mementos: The Chamberlain artefacts too sensitive to exhibit

children.content: Forty years ago, the chance discovery of a bloodied matinee jacket unravelled a murder conviction. Now, that evidence lives inside a national museum's collection that's too sensitive for public display.

image.altText: A black and white photo of a couple outside court, they are holding up a large photo of Lindy Chamberlain holding baby Azaria.

items.title: 'Systemic issue': Abuse sees Canberra referees quitting after a year

children.content: Canberra soccer match officials say referee abuse is a "systemic issue" across all sporting codes, with increasingly hostile behaviour from spectators, players and coaches causing 44 per cent of Capital Football's referees to leave their roles after a year.

items.title: 'Everybody is equal at the table': Inside the ACT's Circle Sentencing court

children.content: A pilot program allowing First Nations offenders to be sentenced by magistrates sitting alongside elders in the ACT Supreme Court secures funding for another three years.

image.altText: A person in a dark shirt stands inside an office.

items.title: How a discarded coffee cup helped solve a 24-year cold case killing

children.content: Twenty-four years after the killing of a Canberra grandmother, a coffee cup discarded on an east coast road trip leads to the arrest and convictions of her killers.

image.altText: An older man and woman sitting on a bench and smiling.

items.title: 'Insurance policy' hoping to protect quolls if bird flu arrives in ACT

children.content: A population of endangered eastern quolls is being protected by a sanctuary in Canberra, but the threat of bird flu could mean that protection does not go far enough.

articles.title: World Cup quick hits: Salah's Socceroos boost and the tightest call

children.content: The Socceroos' next World Cup opponents Egypt are sweating on the fitness of key striker Mohamed Salah, Scotland's coach Steve Clarke resigns after his team's exit and Davinson Sánchez was denied a late winner because of his toe. Here are today's quick hits.

image.altText: Offside, Mo Salah and Steve Clarke composite.

articles.title: Pre-made plans falter as England labours again

children.content: England came into the World Cup with a side it thought was capable of beating the best teams in the world. If it can't beat the little guys, it may never get a chance to prove its theory right.

articles.title: History beckons but a 'fearless' opponent awaits the Socceroos

children.content: Egypt is sweating on the fitness of star forward Mohamed Salah, but the Socceroos won't be complacent as they prepare for just the third FIFA World Cup knockout encounter in the team's history.

items.title: Shark-spotting drones to monitor 70 NSW beaches every day of the year

items.title: How many products in your daily life are made from fossil fuels?

items.title: 'Very thankful': Mum of child with amputation grateful for community support

children.content: Braxton's mum says there needs to be more clothing options available for children with amputations, after struggling to find some for her son whose leg became amputated in the womb.

items.title: Like many war heroes, the man who won this medal never talked about it

children.content: Private Michael Paice never talked about his distinguished medal from World War II, but his story of extraordinary bravery is now being told.

items.title: As the cost of living rises, old-school bartering makes a comeback

children.content: Bartering is one of the oldest trade systems in the world, and as the cost of living soars, there is growing interest in trading homegrown and homemade goods.

items.title: Wallabies and All Blacks locked in for 123-year Bledisloe Cup first

children.content: For the first time in 123 years, the Wallabies and All Blacks will play a Bledisloe Cup Test on Anzac Day, with Lang Park hosting in 2027, 2029 and 2031.

items.title: Autistic talent tapped to help fill growing cybersecurity skills gap

children.content: The cybersecurity sector is projected to need tens of thousands of roles filled by 2030. Now, a university-based program is helping provide those workers by tapping into the abilities of autistic people.
